content:
1. Introduction
Electrodialysis (ED) is a widely used electrically driven membrane process for solution desalination, concentration and separation of ions [1, 2]. The main economic advantage of this technique is that during ED ions are removed from the feed solution. In pressure-driven ion separation and concentration processes, the solvent water passes through the membrane. Therefore, the costs of ED process decrease approximately proportionally to the decrease in ion concentration, while in reverse osmosis (RO) and nanofiltration (NF) these costs only slightly depend on solution concentration [3]. Consequently, ED is a significantly less energy consuming process than RO and NF, when the feed solution concentration is sufficiently low [3, 4]. Recent estimations made by Patel, Biesheuvel and Elimelech [5] show that the critical value of total dissolved solids in the feed solution is 3 g L⁻¹. Another advantage is the ability to obtain highly concentrated solutions and high rates of water recovery, which is not possible in the case of using only RO and NF [6, 7]. With a process similar to ED using capacitive flow electrodes, one can even realize deep desalination and high concentration of solutions [8,9]. However, when treating feed solution towards deep desalination, there is a serious limitation of ED: ionic fluxes through the membranes do not follow the driving force but are limited by the diffusion from the bulk electrolyte through laminar boundary layers to the membrane/electrolyte solution interface, well-known as concentration polarization [10]. This leads to a significant increment of electrical resistance in the diluate compartment, which results in an increase in the ratio of the energy consumption to the minimum required by thermodynamics [5]. To overcome this challenge, narrow ED diluate channels [11], ion-exchange resin fillers [12] and ion-conductive spacers within diluate channels (electrodeionisation) [10] and other methods [13] are used. A phenomenon, that various authors designate as a possible means of overcoming this limitation, is electroconvection [14–17]. Electroconvection (EC) is generally understood as a current-induced volume transport, when an electric field is imposed through the charged solution [18]. This volume transport gives rise to microvortices at the depleted membrane/electrolyte solution interface. The vortices mix the solution near the surface and bring fresh concentrated solution from the bulk solution and delivers it to the membrane surface. Simultaneously, it reinjects the depleted solution from the membrane surface to the bulk [19–21].

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content: Unstable electroconvection producing the vortices with the size comparable with the diffusion layer thickness has been first mathematically predicted in 2000 [14], and later experimentally verified in 2008 [22]. More details of the chaotic turbulence even at zero Reynolds number have been quantified recently through systematic computational visualization experiments [23]. EC increases mass transfer rate even when one operates in conventional ED stack configuration. Experiment shows [15] that due to reducing the area of expensive ion-exchange membranes, it is economically advantageous to apply overlimiting currents (OLC) when treating dilute solutions. Over the last ten years, various new configurations of ED stacks where EC is effectively used for enhancing mass transfer rate have been proposed and intensively discussed. These new systems include 'ion concentration polarization (ICP) desalination', which uses cation-exchange membranes and microporous membranes as splitters [11, 19], 'shock ED' [25–30], applying also cation-exchange membranes but with a porous material filling the space between them. Other microelectrodialysis [31] and microfluidic [32–35] devices are described. The latter uses the EC phenomenon for electrophoresis, in particular for DNA sequencing [36], electroosmotic pumps [37–39] and others [40–42]. This introduction shows that the use of EC is considered as one of the possible routes to improve large-scale electrodialysis as well as nano-microfluidic processes. Its origin, the complex interplay of dynamic ion concentration gradients with space charge gradients causes volumetric forces moving the liquid in chaotic patterns depending on the potential difference over the fluid to be deionized. In this paper, we answer the question whether the spatio-temporal space charges at the membrane–fluid interface may interact or even eliminate each other when oppositely charged ion exchange membranes operated in close vicinity and when opposite space-charges interact with each other in time and space.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content:
2. Background
Electromembrane mass transport processes involving EC are mathematically complex since the local deviation from electrical neutrality and coupling between ion and water transport must be taken into account. Modern theories of electrodialysis are based on a two-dimensional model by Sonin and Probstein [43,44], named by the authors hydrodynamic theory of ED, known also as convection-diffusion model [45]. According to this model, when an electrolyte solution flows between an AEM and a CEM in the absence of a spacer, a concentration profile with a clearly pronounced plateau in the center of the channel and diffusion layers at the membrane surface are established at a small distance from the channel entrance. It is the “developing” region of the channel. At a sufficiently large distance, in the “developed” region, the diffusion layers overlap and fill the entire channel. The concentration profile is not symmetric for the electrolytes containing cation and anion with different mobility in solution (as in the case of NaCl). Since the mobility of the Cl⁻ ion is higher than that of Na⁺, the current density, which is limiting for Na⁺, is underlimiting for Cl⁻. At this current density, the electrolyte concentration reaches a close to zero value at the CEM, but not at the AEM. The most interesting is the “developing” region with a small thickness of diffusion layers; the use of spacers [3,46–48] or membranes with profiled [49] and patterned surface [17,50–52] allows enhancing mass transfer and reducing the effective value of diffusion layer thickness. In the practice of ED, only this “developing” region occurs because the presence of spacers imposes periodic disturbance of the developing flow.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content: As for the shape of concentration profile, experiments indeed show that when a relatively small voltage/current density is applied in a spacer-free feed channel, the concentration profiles characteristic to the “developing” region are observed. A diffusion layer with a total thickness δtot, where the concentration changes occur, is clearly identified. This layer includes the Nernst diffusion boundary layer of thickness δN, which determines the concentration gradient at the membrane surface and the value of ion flux through the membrane, in particular, that of the limiting current density [53]. δN is the thickness of the zone where diffusion is the rate-determining mechanism of ion transfer. However, in the overlimiting current regime, the distribution of concentrations differs significantly from the classical theory [43]. The emergence of EC vortices at i>ilim leads to flattering the concentration profile near the membrane surface: an electroconvection-dominated zone of thickness d appears within the diffusion layer. With that, the diffusion-dominated zone is shifted away from the membrane/solution boundary; the thickness δ'N of this zone decreases with increasing current density, while δtot increases [21]. When the current is many times higher than the limiting one, and the voltage is of the order of 10 V per cell pair, a near-chaotic state occurs: the concentration distribution becomes non-monotonous, enriched and depleted regions alternate in the channel space.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content: There are a number of theoretical publications describing overlimiting current electrodialysis. Most of them use a 2D mathematical description applying the Nernst-Planck-Poisson and Navier-Stokes equations. At relatively low voltages, electroconvection occurs as electroosmosis of the first kind [54–57] or (quasi-)equilibrium electroosmosis [58–60]. In this case, the solution near the membrane surface comes into motion under the action of the applied electric field on the (quasi-)equilibrium space charge region (SCR) (electrical double layer, EDL). The latter exists even in the absence of the external electric field and only slightly depends on the applied current/voltage. In this mode of EC, no visible vortices are visualized, and no significant effect on mass transfer is expected. However, when the current density exceeds its limiting value and an extended SCR appears, the thickness of which is much larger than that of the EDL, significant vortices emerge. They can essentially increase the mass transfer rate, as it was first theoretically predicted by Rubinstein and Zaltzman [14]. This EC mode is named electroosmosis of the second kind [54,55] or non-equilibrium electroosmosis [14,56,58]. The dynamics of formation of EC vortices has been extensively studied [61,62]. While in the absence of forced flow, the vortices are paired and have a close size [14,63], the forced flow reduces the size of the vortices rotating so that their tangential velocity at a relatively large distance from the membrane is opposite to that of the shear flow. The vortices can be drawn by the tangential shear flow. The formation of vortex clusters, in which paired vortices rotate in opposite directions with different velocities, was described by Urtenov et al. [64]. Mani and co-workers [17,20] studied the transition of EC vortex structure from regular coherent individual vortices to chaotic multiscale patterns, which are accompanied by current oscillations.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content:
3. 1D Model
3.1. Model formulation
To address the above questions, we will consider two mathematical models, a 1D and a 2D model, accompanied by new experimental visualization of the spatio-temporal patterns of concentration distribution in a membrane system in an intensive current regime. Using the 1D model, we consider a motionless solution between two membranes and indicate the space-charge breakdown phenomenon as the mutual annihilation of two oppositely charged SCRs, which are formed at the CEM and AEM. With the 2D model, we examine how the emergence of the vortices and the forced convection affect the breakdown phenomenon, and inversely, how the breakdown influences the vortex structures.
First, we introduce a 1D model for instructional reasons and to establish the observation of potential space charge breakdown in a hypothetical 1D geometry. In this model, an immobile electrolyte solution is considered between an AEM (on the left) and CEM (on the right). The system can be interpreted as a desalination channel, which is closed to the inlet and outlet. A (conduction) electric current of density IF flows from the left to the right. The model involves the material balance equation, the Nernst-Planck and Poisson equations, as well as the equation expressing IF as the sum of the contributions from the individual ion flux densities. Eqs. (1)-(4) are written for the case of 1:1 electrolyte composed of a cation (subscript 1) and an anion (subscript 2) with the charge numbers zi equal to +1 and -1, respectively; Ci, ji and Di are the concentration, flux density, and the diffusion coefficient of ion i; ε is the dielectric constant of the solution, F is the Faraday constant, R is the gas constant, φ and E = -∂φ/∂x are the electric potential and field strength, respectively. Only two ions, an anion and a cation, are considered; the presence of the H⁺ and OH⁻ ions, as well as water splitting are not taken into account. Modelling the influence of these ions on salt electrolyte transport and occurring of water splitting can be found, e.g. in Ref. [74,75].

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content:
The boundary conditions assume that: 1) the concentration of counterion, Cim (i=1 and 2 for cation and anion, respectively), on the membrane surface (at x=0, the AEM surface, and at x=H, the CEM surface) is known (the Rubinstein–Shtilman boundary condition [76]; Cim should be lower than the concentration of the fixed ions in the membrane, but essentially higher than the minimum concentration of counterion, Cis, at the membrane surface. 2) the co-ion flux through the membrane/solution interface is zero, both membranes are impermeable to coions; 3) the potential difference between x=0 and x=H, Δφ(t), is a known function of time; it is accepted that Δφ(t) = -d·t, where d is the rate of the potential difference growth; in the calculation reported in this section, d = 10 mV s⁻¹. The value of d was chosen so that the calculated I-V curve was quasi-stationary. 4) at t=0, the electroneutrality condition is satisfied at all points, and the concentrations of counterion and coion are equal to the initial concentration of the electrolyte, C0; the potential is zero everywhere.
3.2. Concentration and space charge density profiles
The boundary value problem (1-7) is solved numerically using COMSOL Multiphysics 5.5. The most calculations are carried out for the cases of NaCl and KCl. Fig. 3 shows the results of calculation for the distribution concentrations of counterion and coion in the solution between the membranes for both electrolytes as a function of time when the potential difference grows in time. In the case of NaCl the electrolyte concentration decreases more rapidly near the CEM due to the greater mobility/diffusion coefficient and transport number of Cl⁻ ions compared to those of Na⁺ ions. The rate of decrease of ion concentration at the membrane surface is proportional to the difference in the transport numbers of the counterion in the membrane and solution. Since the counterion transport number in the membrane is close to 1 (especially in dilute solutions) and tCl > tNa, the difference is lower for the CEM in NaCl solutions. Consequently, the limiting current density, Ilim, for the CEM is lower than that for the AEM. The current density in the system is the same for both membranes, hence, the I/Ilim ratio as well as the degree of the boundary solution concentration polarization is higher for the CEM than for the AEM. In the case of KCl for which t≈0.5, the concentration profiles are symmetrical.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content: In the central part of the desalination channel, the local electroneutrality condition is satisfied, and the concentrations of cation and anion are the same with high accuracy [74]. Within the model of Sonin and Probstein [43], this condition is applied to the entire channel. However, in our model, we take into account that the concentrations of counterion and coion vary continuously. The concentration of counterion is low near the membrane and increases rapidly when approaching the membrane to reach its concentration in the membrane close to the concentration of the fixed ions. The concentration of coion decreases rapidly near the membrane. Due to the difference in the concentrations of counterion and coion, a space charge is formed in the vicinity of the membrane surface. The space charge density, ρ, is defined as ρ = F(C1 - C2). Figs. 4a-4h show the distribution of the space charge density in the solution for different times during the potential sweep. In the space charge region (SCR) near the CEM, cations prevail over anions; in the SCR near the AEM, anions predominate. The concentration of counterion has a minimum point, which separates the SCR into two zones [74]: the quasi-equilibrium zone next to the membrane surface, and the extended SCR zone adjacent to the electroneutral solution in the central channel part. The minimum concentration of counterion, Cis, decreases with increasing current density. At a fixed concentration in the bulk solution, the Cis value decreases only slightly with increasing current and remains of the order of a small parameter ε = LD/H, where LD is the Debye length. The latter is the ratio of the Debye length to the characteristic length of the system. However, the bulk electrolyte concentration in the non-flowing desalination channel (considered here) decreases with time. In these conditions, Cs decreases quite noticeably with increasing current. Note that at the point where the counterion attains its minimum and in some vicinity of this point, the concentration of coion is negligible. Therefore, in this region, the space charge density is approximately ρ ≈ zi F Cis, and |ρ| reaches its minimum at the same point as the concentration of counterion.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content:
3.3. Space charge breakdown in 1D system
The maximum point on the profile of the space charge density modulus (|ρ|) appears when the current density rises above its limiting value for a given membrane and an extended zone of the SCR emerges. In the case of NaCl, a local maximum of |ρ| at the CEM appears at |Δφ|=0.87 V; at the AEM, this maximum emerges at |Δφ|≈1.0 V. With increasing time (and the potential difference), the local maxima move away from the “native” membranes towards the opposite membrane. These extended SCRs behave like two symmetrical solitary waves (or solitons [74,79]). During the potential sweep, the positions of the maxima of the space charge densities formed at the CEM and AEM are reproached. When the distance between the edges of the positive and negative SCRs becomes sufficiently short, they begin to attract each other, and the speed of their approach increases. When touching each other, they “annihilate”, and the space charge breakdown occurs. From the moment of touching to complete “annihilation”, when the SCRs vanish at both membranes, about 1 second passes.
3.4. Faradaic (conduction) and charging currents
Total current density IT in a circuit is a sum of the faradaic (or conduction) current density, IF, and the charging current density IC = ε ∂E/∂t [80,81]: IT = IF + IC. It follows from the material balance and Poisson equations that both IF and IC can change with the distance during the formation of SCRs in the solution between the membranes, as ∂ρ/∂t ≠ 0. However, the total current density does not vary with x. Fig. 5 shows the time dependence of the conduction and charging current densities near the AEM, in the channel center and near the CEM. The difference between IF at different points is negligible, however, IC in the channel center at t close to 60 s is nearly zero, while it is significant next to the membranes, since the SCRs are formed there up to approximately 30 s. Then the charge of SCRs slowly decreases due to the decrease in the electrolyte concentration. IF initially increases, since the potential difference increases with time. However, starting from about 10 s, IF decreases as the electrolyte concentration and, consequently, the solution conductivity reduces. With decreasing the distance between the extremums of the SCRs of opposite sign of charge, the charge density near the membranes decreases, so that the charging current at these points becomes negative. When the waves of SCRs reach the channel center, IC at x=0.5H first increases sharply, then decreases even faster (during 1 s): the space-charge breakdown occurs at t≈86 s. With that, the conduction current being already very small at t=85.5 s vanishes at t=86.5 s: all charge carriers are removed from the channel bulk. The latter is experimentally unrealistic as water splitting could also occur as present in the bipolar junction which would replenish the ionic environment. However this 1D Model indicates the possible existence of a charge breakdown and motivates to expand the modelling into the 2D space with flow through a feed channel between two oppositely charged membranes.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content:
4. 2D model
4.1. Description of the model
The 2D model used in this study corresponds to the model features described in detail in Ref. [64]. A salt solution flows between anion-exchange and cation-exchange membranes. A set of the Nernst-Planck-Poisson and Navier-Stokes coupled equations is used. The electrostatic body force density fe = ρ·E = -ρ·∇φ acting on the SCR and causing the coupled convective transport of the solution (electroconvection) is taken into account in the Navier-Stokes equation. The boundary conditions assume continuity of fluxes through the solution/membrane interfaces, the coion fluxes through the interfaces are set zero. As in the case of 1D model and following other authors [63,71,82], the concentrations of counterion, Cim, on the membrane surface (C2m at x=0, the AEM surface, and C1m at x=H, the CEM surface) are set as parameters equal to C0 in the considered here cases. The no-slip condition (the fluid velocity is zero at x=0 and x=H) is applied. The fluid viscosity is taken equal to 8.94×10⁻⁴ Pa s. The input concentration of the electrolyte (assumed electrically neutral), C0=0.01 mol m⁻³, at the channel entrance (y=0) is given. The potential difference between x=0 and x=H is set as a known function of time: Δφ(t) = -d·t, where d is the sweep rate of |Δφ| growth; in the cases considered below, d = 5 mV s⁻¹. The value of d was chosen to satisfy the condition of quasi-stationarity of the ED process: at d ≤ 5 mV s⁻¹, the shape of CVC does not change. Therefore, the computed CVC is the same as that which can be obtained in a stationary state: each current density, I, refers to a constant potential difference that does not evolve with time. We will see, however, that a small change in Δφ after reaching a certain threshold value (corresponding to a current density exceeding Ilim) leads to a significant change in I, which is associated with the unstable state of the system.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content:
C0=0.01 mol m⁻³ is a very low concentration; the concentration of salts in drinking water is about 10 mol m⁻³. Such a low input concentration is taken to facilitate computations, which are very costly for this kind of mathematical problems [67]. At higher concentrations, the thickness of the quasi-equilibrium space charge layer on the membrane surface (electrical double layer) is very thin, which leads to very large concentration gradients and the necessity to apply extremely fine computation grid. The computational costs are often assessed [67] by the value of a dimensional Debye length (or dimensionless electrostatic screening length) ε = LD/H, where LD = sqrt(εε0 RT/(2F²C0)) is the Debye length, and H is the distance between the membranes forming the channel. The smaller ε, the greater the costs. The value of LD for C0=0.01 mol m⁻³ is about 100 nm, H=500 μm; therefore, ε = 2×10⁻⁴. This value is small, the use of ε = 10⁻³ is considered almost as the least possible [67]. As in section 3 and earlier [64], COMSOL Multiphysics 5.5 is applied for computations. The required computing time for our work was 321 hours 59 minutes 26 seconds, the processor is 24 CPUs x 2.50 GHz Intel XEON E5-2695v2 and 112 GB of RAM memory used for computing. We used 45865 mesh elements in this model. The grid thickened near the membranes, at the inlet and outlet, and remained rather coarse in the region of electroneutrality.
The formation of the SCR and the EC vortices is of particular interest for this 2D model. Can the space charge breakdown be observed as in the case of the simplified 1D model? How does the presence of vortex structures affect a potential space charge breakdown phenomenon (the electric current chooses low-resistance pathways in the space, similar as in the case of lightning)? Or vice versa, how does the breakdown affect the vortex structures? Does the degree of chaotic flow motion decrease? Are the sizes and number of EC vortices affected?

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content:
4.2. Current-voltage characteristic
The current-voltage characteristic (CVC) of the membrane system in the case of NaCl solution is computed using the described 2D model (Fig. 7). The CVC can be divided in 6 different regions according to the features in system behavior, in particular, depending on the vortex flow patterns. In the first section, where Δφ varies from 0 to approximately 0.1 V, a close to linear increase in the current density occurs. This region is often called “ohmic”. The second section is the (inclined) plateau of limiting current (from about 0.2 V to 0.65 V). Ion transport by diffusion attains its maximum at about 0.2 V and the CVC slope in this region is due to the fact that the local limiting current density is reached non-simultaneously on the entire surface of the membrane. A possible competing mechanism of current increase in the inclined plateau of limiting current can be electroosmosis of the first kind [54–57] (equilibrium electroosmosis [59,60]). Curve (2) in Fig. 7 shows the CVC calculated in conditions where EC does not occur (viscosity increased 10 times). It can be seen that in region 2 curve (2) coincides with curve (1), computed with taken into account electroconvection; therefore, the contribution of electroconvection in this region (electroosmosis of the 1-st kind) is negligible.
A sharp increase in the current density and the appearance of oscillations on the CVC at ∼0.625 V correspond to the appearance of the first unstable electroconvective vortices along the entire length of the CEM (Fig. 8). Electroconvection in region 3 is classified as non-equilibrium electroconvection (electroosmosis of the 2-nd kind or bulk electroconvection [56]). In section 3 of CVC, unstable visible vortices at the AEM do not yet appear, since the limiting current density through the AEM is approximately 1.5 times higher than through the CEM, due to the fact that DCl ≈ 1.5 DNa. At Δφ≈1.25 V, a new sharp increase in current density is seen in the CVC, it is the beginning of region 4 (Fig. 7). This increase corresponds to the emergence of unstable vortices at the AEM (Fig. 9). At first, the vortices near the CEM and AEM do not interact with each other (section 4), but at Δφ≈1.65 V and greater voltages they collide and the vortex zones near both membranes overlap (Fig. 10) [71]; these events occur in region 5 of the CVC. At ∼2.0 V, the first isolated cases of “global” space charge breakdown take place (Fig. 9). Region 6, Δφ>2.4 V, corresponds to the chaotic interaction of electroconvective vortices with each other at both the CEM and AEM, in this region one can observe quite frequent (about once a second) repeated “global” space-charge breakdowns (Fig. 11). These breakdowns lead to a decrease in the size and number of EC vortices.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content:
4.3. Space-charge breakdown in 2D system
Figs. 10b and 10c show the level curves and surface of the (C1-C2) function between the AEM and CEM in a desalination channel. This function is the space charge density divided by F. A hill, representing a local maximum of the space charge density, is seen in the middle of the channel, closer to the AEM. This maximum originates from the maximum of the extended SCR at the CEM. As in the 1D model, the maximum moves from the CEM to the AEM when time/voltage increases. At the AEM, there is a local minimum of the negative space charge density. However, in the case of NaCl its absolute value is significantly lower than that at the CEM due to the fact that ilim through the AEM is about 1.5 times greater than that through the CEM. Therefore, we observe the transition of the positive space charge from the CEM membrane to the AEM. The negative space charge formed at the AEM moves also from this membrane towards the CEM (as can be seen in Figs. 4e-4g), however, it is not seen in Figs. 10 and 11 as the near-surface solution regions are not shown because of a high difference in the concentration values in these regions and in the middle of the channel. When two space charge regions meet, one positively charged and the other negatively charged, they break down.
With increasing time, in the 2D desalination channel, like in the case of 1D system, the voltage increases while the concentration decreases. Both changes lead to increasing the distance of the concentration of counterion extremums from the corresponding membrane. Therefore, with increasing time, the two extremums of space charge density, the positive one and the negative one, move towards each other, as in the case of 1D process. The difference is in the presence of the forced convection and electroconvection in the 2D channel. Both kinds of convection, especially their interactions, add a factor of randomness, which is amplified by the instability of the SCR with an extremum of space charge density in the bulk of the ED channel.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content: During space-charge breakdown, the space charge absolute values in the channel bulk (outside of the quasi-equilibrium regions of the EDL) decreases (Fig. 12a), like in the case of 1D model (Fig. 4). This leads to decreasing the driving force for electroconvection. As a consequence, the EC vortices become reduced in the region where the “annihilation” takes place, and the local current density across the membrane surface in the vicinity of this region decreases (like as in 1D model, Fig. 5a). The state just before the discharge is shown in Fig. 10. A red band presenting the elevated positive charge density extends from the CEM to the AEM. The discharge occurs at the AEM between 594.5 s and 595 s. Fig. 12a shows that at 595 s there is a local maximum of the space charge density at a distance x≈100 μm from the AEM; at 595.5 s this maximum disappears. The normal component of the current density, Ix, in the section y=1450 μm, decreases gradually starting at 594 s and reaching the minimum at 595 s when the discharge occurs (Fig. 12b). A vortex, initially presented in the vicinity of the discharge, shifts towards the CEM (Figs. 13a and 13b), which should cause the decrease in Ix at y=1450 μm. However, soon after the discharge, at 595.5 s, there is a sharp increase in Ix (Fig. 12b). Although at y=1450 μm and 60<x<350 μm, the space charge density is nearly zero, the increase in current density can be due to a newly appeared vortex at the AEM with the center at y≈1350 μm (Fig. 13c). As the above consideration shows, relatively high values of the space charges formed near the oppositely charged membranes and a small distance between the membranes create conditions for their “annihilation”: the convergence of the centers of these oppositely charged space charges invariably ends with their mutual elimination. As a consequence, the rate of electroconvection and mass transfer in the vicinity of space-charge breakdown decreases. Although the current density can locally increase in the section where the charge breakdown occurred, this phenomenon is repeated in another section. Therefore, the phenomenon of space-charge breakdown determines the upper limit of the increase in the mass transfer rate due to electroconvection.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content:
4.4. Concentration and space-charge density patterns
At high voltages and low concentrations, the patterns of concentration and space charge fields are rather complicated. The regions of high and low concentration, high and low charge density alternate in the 2D channel. Moreover, one can see that there are bands of high and low space charge density, which stretch from one membrane to another and which alternate in the longitudinal direction (Fig. 11). Space charge breakdown occurs along the routes of relatively high conductivity, not necessarily normal to the membrane surface (Fig. 11). There are similarities to lightning, that is the electrostatic discharge of two electrically charged regions in the atmosphere or ground: the charge of these two regions must be great enough, while the distance between them small; the discharge passes through domains of relatively high conductivity. However, the duration of lightning is very fast, while a space-charge breakdown lasts about 1 s, the time is controlled by ion electrodiffusion at a distance of about 1/10 of the distance between the membranes; the lightning discharge current is very high, while during space-charge breakdown in membrane system, there is no increase in the value of conductance current (Fig. 5a), but a splash in the charging current is observed (Fig. 5b). In both cases, space-charge breakdown ends with a decrease in current density (Figs. 5a, b) and abatement of coupled effects (such as electroconvection in membrane channel, Fig. 13).
The computation shows that negatively charged regions emerge near the CEM at sufficiently high voltages, regions 5 and 6 in CVC, Figs. 9 b,c and Figs. 10 b,c. Since the surface of a CEM is charged negatively, this is a rather unexpected effect. In a sense, it is the reverse of the space charge breakdown. This effect is apparently caused by the local action of several factors: electromigration, diffusion and convection (forced and electroconvection) in conditions of a strong external electric field. Although this effect is absent in the case of an immobile solution (the 1D model, Fig. 4), it is difficult to imagine that it can be caused only by the interactions of EC vortices. The interactions between EC vortices and forced convection seems to be important. A certain support for this can be the results of visualization of the vortex patterns in the ED channel with a forced flow (Fig. 13).

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content:
Fig. 14 and video in Supplementary Information show that an “ejection” of a portion of the depleted solution is possible at a sufficiently large distance (several mm) from the membrane/depleted solution interface. Depleted solution is produced in a very thin layer (~10 μm) near the membrane. A portion of such solution can be picked up by a rotating vortex at the membrane surface and carried out at a distance equal to the diameter of the vortex. Further, this portion is caught by the forced flow of the solution, which flows around the vortex cluster being an obstacle to the forced flow. When flowing around vortex structures, the normal component of the forced flow appears, which is directed towards the opposite (upper) membrane. Near this membrane, the solution is concentrated and vortices are not formed. As a result, a portion of the depleted solution can be carried over distances that are two to three times larger than the maximum size of vortex structures (about 500 μm in the system under consideration, Fig. 14).
The mechanism of the emergence of a negatively charged region near the CEM surface is a rather complicated issue; it might be associated with the existence and properties of self-similar solutions of the system of Nernst-Planck-Poisson-Navier-Stokes equations. This topic requires a separate study.
Space-charge breakdown in the experimentally studied system is not possible, since both membranes forming the considered channel are anion-exchange membranes. In addition, the distance between the membranes and the concentration of the feed solution are too great. According to calculations, at a feed solution concentration of 10⁻⁵ mol L⁻¹, an intermembrane distance of 500 μm, and a solution flow rate of 0.1 mm s⁻¹, the breakdowns begin at a voltage of 1.74 V. At present, computational difficulties do not allow us to say how an increase in the concentration of the feed solution will affect the occurrence of the space-charge breakdown phenomenon. In real systems, it is difficult to detect such a phenomenon in the concentration range of the order of 10⁻⁵ mol L⁻¹, as used in calculations, due to the experimental difficulties in realizing a reproducible quasi-stationary process of ED desalination of a very dilute solution. The development of the scenario described by the idealized model applied here can be hindered by side effects: ion exchange between the solution and the membrane, as well as generation of H⁺ and OH⁻ ions (water splitting) at the solution / membrane boundaries. The impact of these effects on the space-charge breakdown requires additional research.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS

content:
5. Conclusion
Numerical simulation of the ED of solution desalting in the channels formed by anion and cation exchange membranes is reported. During the electrodialysis of dilute solutions at sufficiently high voltages and low rates of forced solution flow, a phenomenon of space-charge breakdown through the solution in the desalting chamber emerges. Under the above conditions, a negative space charge forms near the anion-exchange membrane, and a positive one near the cation-exchange membrane. The curves of the distribution of both negative and positive space charge density in the intermembrane solution have characteristic extrema at some distance from the membrane surface. With an increase in the voltage and a decrease in concentration, these extrema, initially formed near the membrane's surfaces, approach each other. Ultimately, this ends with a discharge of two space charges of opposite signs. The moment of breakdown is preceded by a high degree of instability of the EC vortex motion, which leads to a high degree of randomness in the distribution of concentration, solution flow rates, and space charge density. Immediately after breakdown, the space charge density near both membranes drops to almost zero locally along the breakdown line. Accordingly, this leads to a sharp decrease in the EC vortex motion in the region of discharge and to a decrease in the local current density.
The breakdown phenomenon described in this theoretical work is a possible scenario for the development of the ED process of desalination at high voltages and low salt concentrations. The observation of such a scenario in real systems can be overshadowed by other simultaneously occurring phenomena: ion exchange between the solution and the membrane, and water splitting at the solution / membrane interface. Taking into account the influence of these effects on the process of ED desalination in intensive current regimes represents yet another herculian effort in unravelling the physical details of overlimiting currents.

citation.title: SPACE-CHARGE BREAKDOWN PHENOMENON AND SPATIO-TEMPORAL ION CONCENTRATION AND FLUID FLOW PATTERNS IN OVERLIMITING CURRENT ELECTRODIALYSIS
